bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the conflict as a "war of choice," emphasizing "no evidence" for Trump's claims and questioning the administration's justification.

Conversely, right-leaning coverage highlights Trump's pledge to "avenge" "fallen" service members, portraying the conflict as a controlled "operation" unfolding "as planned" and even mentioning new leadership talks.

Center perspectives neutrally report Trump's "four-week" timeline prediction and analyze economic impacts like "Oil Phantom" volatility, de-emphasizing the left's skepticism and the right's nationalistic resolve.

While all acknowledge the "four weeks" duration and "three American service members killed," the left frames "casualties/losses" somberly, the right heroically, revealing deep ideological divides over military intervention and presidential authority.
summary: On Feb. 28, 2026, President Donald Trump oversaw Operation Epic Fury, launched Saturday morning, and said the campaign would last `four weeks, or less`.
Officials said the strikes aimed to remove Iran's senior leadership and degrade missile and drone networks, framing the operation as retribution after three U.S. service members were killed.

U.S. and Israeli officials said the strikes killed Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and about 40 senior Iranian officials, destroyed nine Iranian naval ships and a naval headquarters, and hit three Tehran locations within 60 seconds.
The State Department announced it is reducing its diplomatic footprint and advising non-essential staff to leave Bahrain and Qatar as U.S.-Israeli strikes and Iranian retaliation continue.

A senior White House official said Iran's new leadership signaled openness to talks while President Donald Trump said he is `eventually` willing to talk, Oman’s Foreign Minister Badr al-Busaidi urged negotiations to resume, and a Reuters/Ipsos poll found 27% support, 43% oppose and 29% unsure.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning outlets portray the UK's decision to allow US use of bases as Starmer having "relented" after "previously refused" requests, with some accusing him of "contradicting himself" and referencing "the mistakes of Iraq.
Conversely, right-leaning coverage frames the move as the US being "cleared to use" bases, with the UK "jump into the fray" due to a "near miss" and Starmer's "duty" to protect citizens.
Center-Leaning reports neutrally present the authorization.
While the left de-emphasizes recent provocations, the right highlights them as justification.

All perspectives agree on the "defensive purpose" and "protecting British lives," yet diverge on the underlying motivations and historical context, reflecting broader ideological stances on intervention.
summary: The UK has agreed to let the US use British military bases for 'defensive' strikes on Iranian missile sites to 'protect British lives'.
Prime Minister Keir Starmer said British aircraft have successfully intercepted Iranian strikes, but the US requested to use UK bases to 'destroy the missiles at source'.
Starmer stated the UK 'will not join offensive action' against Iran and wants to avoid 'the mistakes of Iraq'.
title: UK will allow US to use bases to strike Iranian missile sites, PM says

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the supreme leader's death as an "assassination," portraying Trump as "applauding" attacks and taking a "victory lap" amidst "chaos" and soaring oil prices, highlighting Iran's prior interest in de-escalation.

Right-Leaning coverage, however, characterizes the leader as "eliminated," presenting Trump as a decisive figure who "stuns world" by agreeing to talks, while also suggesting aggressive options like "eradicate" for Iran's "tyranny.
Center reports neutrally convey Trump's statements and offer more specific casualty claims.
While all agree Trump stated Iran's new leadership wants to talk and he agreed, the contrasting narratives reveal deep ideological divides over US foreign policy and presidential authority.
summary: On March 1, 2026, U.S. President Donald Trump said he agreed to speak with Iran's new leadership, adding `They want to talk, and I have agreed to talk, so I will be talking to them` in a phone interview with The Atlantic from his Florida residence.
Following coordinated strikes, the United States and Israel launched Operation Epic Fury and Operation Roaring Lion in a decapitation campaign after months of escalating tensions.
Asked about timing, Trump said `Most of those people are gone` and told Fox News the strikes killed at least 48 Iranian leaders.
Iran vowed retaliation as Ali Larijani warned the strikes set the nation ablaze, while the U.S. Army announced three US service members killed and five seriously wounded.

Iran has named an interim leadership council led by Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian to serve until a new supreme leader is chosen, while Oman and Indonesia offered mediation as Iran signals openness to serious efforts to stop escalation.
title: Trump agrees to speak to Iran’s interim leadership: Report

summary: U.S. Central Command announced Sunday that three U.S. service members were killed and five wounded during combat operations against Iran, the military said.
The campaign began Saturday with U.S.-Israeli strikes targeting Iran’s leadership and military infrastructure, killing the Supreme Leader, and prompting Iranian retaliations, officials said.
Officials said identities of the dead are withheld for 24 hours after next-of-kin notification, and several troops with minor injuries are being returned to duty.
CENTCOM said major combat operations and its response effort remain ongoing, while airports in Dubai and the region face closures with widespread flight cancellations for March 1.

The strikes have reverberated globally, raising fears of wider conflict and disruptions to international stability, with analysts warning of potential market spikes and the U.S. urging Iranians to `take over`.
title: US Military Says 3 Service Members Killed and 5 Seriously Wounded in Iran Operation

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes potential motives like "Trump's Bombing of Iran," de-emphasizing specific weapons or the suspect's "Property of Allah" clothing, using terms like "shocking scenes.

Right-Leaning outlets, however, frame the event more definitively as "Islamic Terror" or "Lone-Wolf Terror?", highlighting police action against an "armed gunman" and detailing the suspect's attire, often employing emotionally charged language like "Bloodbath.
Center perspectives cautiously report a "potential terror link," providing similar details to the right but with less definitive framing, and uniquely noted a self-driving vehicle incident.
While all agree on the tragic casualties and FBI investigation into a "potential nexus to terrorism," these portrayals reveal ideological divides in attributing blame and emphasizing threats.
summary: On Sunday, March 1, 2026, a shooting at Buford's on West Sixth Street in Austin left three dead, including the suspect, and 14 wounded, officials said.

Austin Police Chief Lisa Davis said the suspect circled the block, fired a handgun from an SUV, parked, then exited and continued shooting with a rifle from outside Buford's in the West Sixth Street entertainment district.
Paramedics and officers arrived within 57 seconds of a 1:39 a.m. call, with more than 20 EMS resources and all critical patients off scene within 24 minutes, Luckritz said.
The FBI's San Antonio office dispatched teams, and officials said evidence processing will take hours as the FBI Joint Terrorism Task Force joins the investigation.
Officials said it's too early to determine a motive, and leaders urged action, with Congressman Greg Casar calling for an end to gun violence.
title: Texas bar shooting leaves 3 dead and 14 wounded as FBI investigates terrorism possibility

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the E3's "defensive action" as "team up to stop" Iran 's "revenge strikes," linking it to "flashes of anger" after US/Israeli actions.

Right-Leaning outlets portray the same as a "Vow to Defend Interests" in a "War Against Iran," employing terms like "appalling strikes" and explicitly mentioning "work with Trump," focusing on "attacking 'in origin' Iran's missile and drone launchers.
Center-Leaning analysis de-emphasizes military confrontation, highlighting calls for a "return to negotiations" and specific details like UK base usage, framing events as "heightened tensions.

While all perspectives acknowledge the E3's readiness for "defensive action" against "retaliatory attacks," they diverge significantly on the emphasis of diplomacy versus aggressive posture, revealing underlying ideological divides on international engagement.
summary: Iran has launched missile and drone strikes on several Gulf countries, targeting US bases after being hit by US-Israeli missile strikes.
The UK, France, and Germany have vowed to take defensive action to destroy Iran's capability to fire missiles and drones at the source.
The three countries' leaders were appalled by Iran's indiscriminate and disproportionate missile attacks against countries in the region.
title: UK, France, Germany vow defensive action against Iran's missile, drone capabilities

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump's reaction to service member deaths as "boasting" about a "great deal" for lives, highlighting "sharp criticism" and calling it an "Iran war .

In stark contrast, right-leaning outlets emphasize "Combat Ops to Continue Until All Objectives Achieved," portraying "major success" and "overwhelming military offenses," even asserting "dozens of top Iranian leaders" were killed and Trump "demands" surrender.
Center coverage neutrally presents Trump's "warns" and direct quotes about "likely more" deaths.
While all acknowledge three US service members killed, the left mentions Israel's involvement, whereas the right discusses a lack of "exit strategy.
These portrayals underscore fundamental ideological differences regarding military action and presidential accountability.
summary: President Donald Trump confirmed on Sunday that three U.S. service members were killed and five wounded during strikes that began early Saturday as a joint U.S.-Israeli operation.
The administration says the strikes were launched because Iran `weren't willing to say they will not have a nuclear weapon` and aimed at `decapitating them` to remove leadership, he said.

Operationally, the president claimed in a Truth Social post that `we have destroyed and sunk 9 Iranian Naval Ships` and said the Naval Headquarters was largely destroyed while overseeing Operation Epic Fury at Mar-a-Lago, Palm Beach, Fla.
Several members urged Congress should have a clearer role, with most Republicans praising the strikes and most Democrats criticizing Trump’s action without congressional approval.
Trump said he `doesn't know` when strikes will stop and that the operation remains uncertain, with no public briefings since his Saturday video.
title: Trump warns 'there will likely be more' US deaths in Iran conflict

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames military action as an "attacked" "unpredictable war ," emphasizing "overwhelmingly oppose" public sentiment and criticizing "Trump's strikes" with terms like "too quick to use force" and even sarcastic framing like "Operation Epstein Diversion.
Right-Leaning outlets, while also noting low public approval for "attacks," often use the more definitive "War Against Iran" and "Trump's new escalation.
Center-Leaning reports remain neutral, presenting poll data like "just one in four Americans" supporting the strikes, avoiding loaded language.

While all perspectives acknowledge the low public approval for the strikes and the occurrence of casualties, the left highlights the choice of "unpredictable war" over diplomacy and political pressure on the administration, revealing a core ideological divide on military intervention and presidential accountability.
summary: The Reuters/Ipsos survey released Sunday found 27% of U.S. adults approved the strikes on Iran, 29% were unsure, and 43% disapproved in a poll of 1,282 adults starting Saturday.
Ahead of the early Saturday, Feb. 28 strikes, the Trump administration cited Iran's imminent threat and ordered military action in Venezuela, Syria and Nigeria amid a U.S. buildup.

Partisan breakdowns showed 56% said President Donald Trump is too willing to use military force, with Democrats at 87% and independents at 60%, while only 27% trusted him overall and support was 40% among Republicans.
title: Just one in four Americans supports US strikes on Iran, Reuters/Ipsos poll finds

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes the "massive assault" and "largest buildup of US warships," portraying the Iranian government as "repressive" while highlighting official skepticism that "regime change" is "unlikely.

Right-Leaning outlets frame the event as an "Attack" and discuss "Regime Change" as a strategic aim, often expressing frustration that it's "Difficult" or "Not Seeing Any Possibility!" Center-leaning reports, in contrast, neutrally present official skepticism, relying on expert analysis to explain *why* regime change is "unlikely" due to the regime's revolutionary origins, de-emphasizing military scale or explicit US aims.

While all perspectives agree on the strikes, Khamenei's death, and official skepticism regarding immediate regime change, their divergent portrayals reveal ideological splits concerning interventionism, military might, and the desirability or feasibility of altering foreign governments.
summary: On Saturday , Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was reported killed in U.S.-Israeli strikes following a massive Feb. 28, 2026 assault.
Three U.S. officials familiar with U.S. intelligence said Iran's opposition is battered by repression and lacks defections from the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps during January protests.

U.S. Central Command announced three American service members were killed and five service members seriously wounded, while Iranian leaders say bombs have killed more than 200 people since the strikes.

President Masoud Pezeshkian confirmed a temporary council of himself, the judiciary head, and a member of the Guardian Council has assumed Supreme Leader duties, while Ali Larijani, security chief, warned of harsh retaliation for secessionist moves.

Agencies have debated with no consensus on whether Khamenei's death shifts Iran's stance on ballistic missile and nuclear negotiations, while President Donald Trump urged Iranians to `I call upon all Iranian patriots who yearn for freedom to seize this moment... and take back your country`.
title: US officials skeptical of regime change in Tehran after Khamenei killing: Reuters

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes the Pentagon 's "no sign" or "no evidence" of a preemptive Iranian attack, framing it as "contradicting key war justifications" and depicting the administration as "construct a case for war.
This perspective often highlights the U.S.-Israeli attacks' simultaneity.

Right-Leaning outlets, while reporting the lack of preemptive intelligence, de-emphasize the broader context of the strikes and instead underscore officials' warnings about ongoing "imminent threat" from Iran's missiles and proxies.
Center-Leaning reports neutrally present the Pentagon's finding, contrasting it with prior presidential statements about "imminent threats.

The core agreement lies in the Pentagon's intelligence assessment, but interpretations diverge sharply on the implications for military action and the administration's motives, reflecting fundamental ideological stances on foreign policy and government accountability.
summary: Trump administration officials privately told congressional staff Sunday that U.S. intelligence did not indicate Iran planned a preemptive strike, officials said.

Administration officials said there was a general regional threat from Iran's missiles and proxy forces, while senior Trump administration officials told reporters Saturday of possible preemptive attack indicators.

Shared intelligence allowed nearly simultaneous daytime strikes, as the CIA tracked senior Iranian leaders for months and three strikes in three locations within a single minute killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and about 40 senior figures.

After reports of U.S. casualties, lawmakers questioned the operation's justification as Trump said Sunday it would continue until 'all of our objectives are achieved,' and three American troops were killed.
On Tuesday, officials will brief Congress, and he said Sunday, `They want to talk, and I have agreed to talk, so I will be talking to them,' referencing Iran's willingness to engage.
title: Pentagon Offers No Evidence to Congress Supporting Claim It Attacked Iran in Self-Defense: Report

bias: Right-Leaning coverage frames the incident as "INSANE," describing the perpetrator as a "crazy man" or "lunatic," emphasizing police were "forced" to act in "self-defense.

Left-Leaning outlets, however, describe a "trooper killed the suspect," while center coverage uses "person armed with knife" and explicitly notes the incident was "not believed to be terrorism-related," a detail de-emphasized elsewhere.
All perspectives agree on the core facts of the stabbings and the trooper's involvement.

These divergent portrayals highlight ideological priorities: the right's focus on moral outrage and police justification versus the left/center's emphasis on descriptive reporting and official assessments.
summary: A Virginia State Police trooper shot an unnamed man around 1:20 p.m. Sunday on the Interstate 495 Beltway at Exit 52 after a confrontation with a knife, police said.
Responding troopers were called about a road-rage incident after a car crash, and the preliminary investigation indicates the stabbings occurred following the crash, Virginia State Police said.
Four people were stabbed, including a 39-year-old woman who died, and a dog was also stabbed and killed, police said.

All southbound lanes were closed Sunday afternoon, with traffic backed up about four miles, while the Virginia State Police Bureau of Criminal Investigation Fairfax Field Office probes the shooting; the trooper was not injured.

Officials said the incident is not believed terrorism-related and remains under investigation, while transportation cameras showed drivers fleeing the backup, including one driving the wrong way; this developing story will be updated.
title: Trooper Kills Person Armed With Knife After Stabbing, Beltway Crash: Police

bias: While all outlets reported Danise Baird's tragic passing from car accident complications, narratives diverged sharply.
Left-Leaning coverage emphasized public sympathy, noting 'condolences poured in' and portraying a life 'centered on faith, family, and service,' de-emphasizing political affiliation.

Conversely, right-leaning outlets highlighted the 'GOP Congressman' and included specific, often graphic details like a 'severe car accident' and 'hit-and-run,' aiming for a stronger emotional impact.
Center-Leaning reports maintained neutrality, presenting facts like the accident location and political party without emotional framing.
This reveals a pivotal divide: personal tragedy is either a universal human story or framed through a political lens, with varying levels of detail and emotional intensity.
summary: On Sunday, Congressman Jim Baird's office announced that Danise Baird, wife of U.S. Rep. Jim Baird, died following complications from injuries sustained in a car accident earlier this year.
Driving to Washington, D.C., the couple's vehicle was struck by another vehicle in early January, and the congressman's office did not disclose what caused the crash or their treatment location.
While Rep. Jim Baird was hospitalized for less than 24 hours with minor injuries, Danise Baird was admitted to the ICU, released a little less than a week later, and died from complications.
Several colleagues offered condolences on social media, including Reps. Pete Stauber, Lisa McClain and Jimmy Patronis, following Danise Baird's death.
The couple had been married for 59 years, and Danise was described as a devoted wife and loving mother of three.
title: Wife of Indiana Rep. Jim Baird dies of ‘complications’ from injuries sustained in January car crash

summary: Next week, eSafety warned it may push search engines and app stores to block AI services that fail age verification after a Reuters review found many had not shown compliance steps.
After the Reuters review, eSafety highlighted concerns about AI using emotional manipulation and noted children as young as 10 using chatbots for up to six hours daily.

Of the 50 most popular text-based AI products, nine had age-assurance plans, 11 planned blanket filters blocking Australians, and Apple said last week it would use “reasonable methods” to restrict minors from 18+ apps .

ESafety said it will use the full range of its powers, including action against gatekeeper services such as search engines and app stores, with fines up to $35 million for failing to restrict under-18s.

Amid growing global scrutiny, eSafety warns it may push app stores and search engines to block AI that lacks age verification, as investigations into Elon Musk’s Grok highlight ongoing probes into AI harms.
title: Australia says it may go after app stores, search engines in AI age crackdown

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes dramatic operational claims—using language like "Operation Epic Fury" and phrases such as "decapitated its leadership" and reported high casualties—framing the story as a geopolitical escalation and raising questions about covert ties, while right-leaning pieces foreground sovereignty and citizen protection, stressing "assisting stranded Australians," "rules out," and practical travel disruptions and portraying non-involvement as prudent; pivotal dividing lines include naming the operation and casualty claims, and whether any non-combat support occurred , areas both sides agree on the government's refusal to join strikes but interpret through different ideological lenses.
summary: On March 2, Australia said it would not take part in any military operations in Iran and ruled out deploying troops if the conflict escalates, Australian Foreign Minister Penny Wong said.
The strikes were carried out by the United States and Israel, with Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei reportedly killed on Saturday as the United States reported its first casualties.
About 115,000 Australians are in the region, and evacuations depend on commercial airlines resuming services while airspace remains closed, with Etihad and Emirates cancelling some flights.
Canberra confirmed it was not told in advance, and Defence Minister Richard Marles said measures protected about 100 Australian defence personnel at Al Minhad Air Base near Dubai.
Wong urged the resumption of dialogue and diplomacy, while official travel advice was upgraded warning Australians not to travel to Israel, Lebanon, Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar and United Arab Emirates.
title: Australia Rules Out Military Role in Iran Conflict

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the operation as "fatal strikes" or "attacks," emphasizing human cost and noting US intelligence did not suggest a preemptive Iranian threat, reflecting a cautious tone.

Right-Leaning outlets, conversely, celebrate a "decisive strike" that "eliminates" Khamenei, using terms like "fateful operation" to convey approval and a hawkish stance, highlighting a "surprise change of movements" as an opportunistic success.
Center-Leaning reports remain neutral, focusing on "tracking" and "locating," providing operational specifics like "30 bombs" or "some 40 senior figures" killed.

While all agree the CIA tracked Khamenei, leading to a joint US-Israeli operation resulting in his death, the divergent portrayals reveal ideological divides between scrutinizing military action and celebrating assertive force.
summary: Intelligence gathered over months by the CIA and shared with Israeli counterparts led to the missile strike that killed Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and other senior Iranian officials on Saturday.
The CIA had been tracking Khamenei's location for several months before Saturday's joint U.S.-Israeli strikes, gaining deeper insight into his whereabouts as he moved around.
The spy agency then learned about a Saturday morning meeting of senior Iranian officials at a compound in Tehran that Khamenei was expected to attend, which accelerated the timeline for the strike.
title: CIA tracked Iranian leaders for months ahead of attacks that began with 3 strikes in 60 seconds

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames U.S./Israeli strikes as "plunging the Middle East into a new war," linking prior oil price hikes to "ramped-up rhetoric" and "ratcheted up sanctions.

Right-Leaning narratives attribute surges to "Iran conflict" or "Iran strikes," detailing "Operation Epic Fury" and an unverified claim about the "death of Iran's supreme leader," also noting Iran's prior Strait of Hormuz actions.
Center perspectives offer a tempered view, including expert predictions of oil prices not exceeding $75-$80.

All perspectives, however, agree on significant oil price surges to around $80 a barrel, with potential for $100, triggered by U.S./Israeli strikes, causing disruptions to global energy supply and shipping routes, and sharing negative sentiment around "disrupt" and "inflation.".
summary: On Sunday, March 1, 2026, Brent crude climbed 13% to $82 a barrel in early trading in Asia after Iran attacked ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz.
Following reports of the killing of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, Iran responded with missile barrages, with analysts saying Iran viewed this as a bid for regime change.

At least three tankers were damaged by missile and drone strikes with one seafarer killed, while about 170 containerships reported delays as owners halted sailings and rerouted Europe-bound vessels around Africa.

Stock futures plunged with S&P 500 futures down 1.1%, Nasdaq 100 futures down 1.2%, and Dow futures falling more than 500 points, while gold rose almost 2% above $5,360 and retail petrol prices could rise 20 cents per gallon soon.

Ajay Parmar of ICIS said, 'We expect prices to open much closer to US$100 a barrel and perhaps exceed that level if we see a prolonged outage of the Strait,' amid Rystad Energy's forecast of prices rising to about US$92 when trade opens, despite OPEC+ raising output by 206,000 barrels from April.
title: Oil prices spike as key shipping route disrupted by Iran attacks
topic_full: Oil and Gas Sector · United States

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Trump's claims of destroying Iranian ships as "bizarrely boasts," implying exaggeration and juxtaposing destruction with "Iran wants to talk," while highlighting U.S. and Iranian casualties.

Right-Leaning coverage, conversely, presents a triumphant narrative, using terms like "declares" and "finished" to describe a "devastating blow," emphasizing "NINE" ships and "satellite pictures" while de-emphasizing casualties.
Center-Leaning outlets neutrally attribute claims, noting Trump's "willingness to talk" and covering the Supreme Leader's death and counterstrikes, which the left de-emphasizes.
All acknowledge the reported U.S. military action, but these portrayals reveal deep ideological divides over military strength, diplomacy, and conflict's human cost.
summary: On March 1, 2026, President Donald Trump announced U.S. forces destroyed and sunk nine Iranian naval ships and largely destroyed Iran’s Naval Headquarters, updating Operation Epic Fury’s opening phase.

On Saturday, Operation Epic Fury commenced with coordinated strikes targeting Iranian command-and-control, air defences, missile and drone launch sites; CENTCOM reported a Jamaran-class corvette struck at a Chabahar pier is sinking, while U.S. forces hit hardened ballistic missile facilities.

CENTCOM reported that Iran’s military retaliated with hundreds of missile and drone attacks, three U.S. service members were killed, five seriously wounded, and B-2 stealth bombers struck hardened ballistic missile facilities Saturday.
Commercial shipping around the Strait of Hormuz is disrupted with at least 150 vessels anchored, while U.S. Central Command urged the IRGC and Iranian forces to `lay down your weapons`.

A temporary Leadership Council including Masoud Pezeshkian and Gholamhossein Mohseni Ejei has taken control, while young Iranians dance and monument toppling signals internal shifts, as analysts warn of risks to Strait of Hormuz shipping.
title: Trump says US strikes have sunk nine Iranian naval ships and hit navy headquarters

bias: Left-Leaning outlets emphasize the "mass shooting" and "chaotic" nature, focusing on two victims and cautiously framing a "potential nexus to terrorism ," explicitly stating investigators de-emphasized a link to Middle East conflicts.
In contrast, right-leaning coverage employs terms like "mass killer" and "rampage," directly labeling the event a "Muslim terrorist attack" and reporting "three dead" by including the gunman.

This perspective heavily highlights the suspect's "Property of Allah" attire, "Iranian flag," naturalization details, and discovery of a Quran or regime imagery, linking the incident to foreign threats and immigration.
Center-Leaning reports, however, neutrally present facts, attributing information to officials, and focus on "extremist motives" and "terrorism-related materials" without definitive labels.

All perspectives agree on the suspect's identity and the FBI's terrorism investigation, yet diverge significantly on the certainty of motive and the emphasis on the suspect's background, reflecting broader ideological splits on national security and identity.
summary: On March 1, 2026, shortly before 2 a.m., gunfire erupted outside Buford's Backyard Beer Garden, prompting officers to confront and shoot the suspect.

The Department of Homeland Security identified the suspect as 53-year-old Ndiaga Diagne, a naturalized U.S. citizen born in Senegal, while FBI agents found indicators prompting a terrorism probe but cautioned it is too early to determine motive.
Images and eyewitness accounts show the suspect wearing a sweatshirt reading `Property of Allah` and an undershirt with Iranian imagery, while authorities found a Quran in his vehicle.
Austin-Travis County EMS Chief Robert Luckritz said three people were killed, including the suspect, and 14 were wounded; first responders arrived within about a minute to treat victims.
The FBI's Joint Terrorism Task Force and ATF joined the investigation, the White House said President Donald Trump was briefed, and Texas Gov. Greg Abbott warned of increased security.
title: Suspect in Texas shooting wore ‘Property of Allah’ clothing with Iranian flag: Associated Press

bias: Left-Leaning coverage neutrally presents the US military 's use of B-2 stealth bombers against Iranian facilities, de-emphasizing broader context or official attribution.

In stark contrast, right-leaning narratives employ triumphalist language, framing the action as an "Unstoppable Stealth" and "Bold Strike" with "No Chance of Stopping," highlighting its "Successful" nature and "Epic Footage.
While all acknowledge the 2,000-pound bombs, the right emphasizes "precision bombs" and "bunker-buster" capabilities, portraying forces as "eliminating imminent threats.
Center-Leaning reports add details like "Operation Epic Fury" and CENTCOM confirmation, noting the US is "going after the rest" of Iran's Navy, and using terms like "pound" for the bombs.
This reveals a fundamental divide in portraying military action: as a basic report versus a decisive, powerful display of national strength.
summary: On March 1, 2026, U.S. Central Command announced B-2 stealth bombers struck Iran 's hardened ballistic missile facilities using 2,000-pound precision bombs and posted related video.
The strikes form part of a broader U.S.-Israeli campaign that began this weekend and has struck more than 1,000 Iranian targets, the Wall Street Journal reported.
The B-2 is built to penetrate defended airspace and conduct long-range precision strikes, with CENTCOM noting this is the first confirmed B-2 use in the current round after June 2025.

Regional bases reported increased tension after the strikes, with Iran saying it had carried out more retaliatory attacks than the U.S. and Israel, while CENTCOM framed the operation as a demonstration of resolve, saying 'No nation should ever doubt America's resolve.'

Use of heavy 2,000-pound munitions suggests high-profile escalation as CENTCOM said B-2 bombers targeted hardened missile facilities, while U.S. President Donald Trump and media reporting emphasized the operation's scale.
title: US military used stealth B-2 bombers to strike Iran’s ballistic missile facilities

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames new Medicaid work mandates by emphasizing potential negative human impacts, highlighting that states will "spend millions" which may cause people to "lose coverage" or "prevent eligible people from accessing coverage," often using terms like "immense task" and "high costs" with a critical tone.
In contrast, right-leaning outlets, while acknowledging the "millions" in spending, portray it as government inefficiency and a fiscal burden, aligning with a fiscally conservative critique.
Center-Leaning reports maintain a neutral tone, presenting the "millions" in spending as a factual implementation challenge without emotional framing.
A pivotal dividing line is the left's focus on the specific "Jan. 1 kickoff" and impact on "millions of lower-income adults," details de-emphasized by the right.

All perspectives agree on the policy's intent to save money and the initial "millions" states must spend, revealing a shared factual baseline but divergent ideological priorities regarding social safety nets versus fiscal prudence.
summary: The Trump administration is implementing new Medicaid work requirements for adults without young children, intended to save $388 billion over a decade and lead to 6 million fewer people insured.
To enact the requirements by January 1, states must spend over $1 billion to upgrade computer systems and hire staff to track participants' work, volunteering or student status.
States will receive $200 million in federal funds to help implement the changes, which require eligibility reviews every six months instead of annually.
title: Trump's Medicaid work mandates are meant to save money. But first states will have to spend millions

bias: Left-Leaning coverage critically frames military action, questioning Trump's "sanity" and linking the crisis to "decades of U.S. meddling" and "forever wars.
Right-Leaning outlets trumpet "unbelievable success," highlighting "48 leaders dead" and "brand new weapon" with a triumphant tone.
Center coverage, while reporting Trump's "ahead of schedule" claim neutrally, uniquely details "200+ dead" and Iran's retaliation.
The pivotal divide centers on leadership accountability and the human cost versus military triumph, connecting to broader ideological stances on intervention.
summary: Yesterday, US President Donald Trump said operations are `ahead of schedule` after US and Israeli forces killed Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, calling the progress `fantastic`.
Israel launched a new wave of strikes on Tehran aiming to dominate the skies and open a path to the capital, while a temporary leadership council assumed governing duties after Khamenei's death.

Retaliatory strikes and raids killed Khamenei's daughter, grandchild, daughter-in-law, son-in-law, and caused civilian deaths including nine in Beit Shemesh, three in the United Arab Emirates, and one in Kuwait.

Travel and trade were disrupted across the region as strikes and Iranian retaliation stranded travellers and crippled airline operations, while shipping and oil sectors faced risks in strategic waterways.
Ground forces are not under consideration, the Israeli military said, while President Donald Trump told Fox News the US military response is `moving along rapidly`.
title: Trump says Iran military operations are 'ahead of schedule,' CNBC reports

bias: News coverage of the lawsuit against Michael Jackson 's estate reveals stark narrative contrasts.
Left-Leaning outlets emphasize the severity of "Child Trafficking" and "Decade-Long Abuse," highlighting victim claims of being "plied with drugs" and "groomed and brainwashed.

Conversely, right-leaning coverage often juxtaposes "s*x trafficking" allegations with the attorney's claim that "clients deserve $200 million," subtly framing the lawsuit around financial gain and employing sensationalized terms like "Drugging and Sexually Assaulting Children," sometimes even including an irrelevant image of Jeffrey Epstein.
Center-Leaning reports, however, maintain neutrality, using qualifiers like "alleged" and "reportedly sue," and providing context about previous settlement efforts.

While all perspectives agree the estate faces a lawsuit from the Cascio family alleging child sex trafficking and abuse, the left prioritizes the alleged systemic harm, while the right scrutinizes motives and sensationalizes, reflecting broader ideological divides on victim advocacy versus legal skepticism.
summary: On Friday , siblings Frank, Dominic, Marie‑Nicole and Aldo Cascio filed a federal complaint in Los Angeles accusing Michael Jackson 's estate and The Michael Jackson Company of child sex trafficking.
Their documents describe how Michael Jackson groomed and isolated the Cascios beginning when some were as young as seven or eight, using gifts and abuse methods.
Reportedly, the complaint details assaults at Neverland Ranch and Elizabeth Taylor's home in Switzerland, with the Cascios previously defending Jackson during his 2005 trial.
A judge has scheduled a follow-up hearing for March 5, as the estate denies the claims and seeks to dismiss the lawsuit, calling it a `desperate money grab`.
Their prior legal strategy included filings after Leaving Neverland , with demands of $40 million and $213 million, and past allegations remain part of the record.
title: Michael Jackson's estate sued over child sex trafficking claims
Rubio Canceled His Trip to Israel Early Next Week Following US-Israel Strikes on Iran, State Department Official Says

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the reported killing of "48 leaders" as a "claim," injecting skepticism with phrases like "No One Can Believe" and highlighting the "Fox News interview" context, while emphasizing an Iranian "power vacuum" and calls for "revenge.

Right-Leaning coverage, conversely, presents the "48 leaders" as "confirmed" and "Have Been Killed," using triumphant language like "wiped out in one shot" and "major success," often detailing additional high-ranking officials killed.
Center-Leaning reports neutrally attribute the "claims," also noting US casualties and strategic goals to "cripple Iran’s nuclear program.
All perspectives acknowledge Trump's statement about the "48 leaders" killed in joint US-Israeli strikes, revealing ideological divides on military action and presidential credibility.
summary: Sunday, United States President Donald Trump said 48 Iranian leaders were killed in joint U.S.-Israeli strikes after the campaign launched Saturday.
Planned for months, U.S. and Israeli intelligence tracked targets and seized a rare Tehran meeting vulnerability on Saturday.

U.S. Central Command reported sinking an Iranian warship at a Gulf of Oman dock and destroying nine Iranian naval ships; CENTCOM later confirmed three American service members killed and five seriously wounded.

Following the strikes, Iranian authorities confirmed the death of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and Iran launched hundreds of missile and drone attacks, closing regional airports including Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi.

Trump said Iran's new leadership signalled willingness to negotiate and that operations are ongoing, with remaining Iranian naval vessels to be sunk soon and the conflict envisaged to last four weeks.
title: Trump says 48 leaders killed in strikes on Iran

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes Israeli military actions like 'attacks' and 'possible strikes' with neutral language, referring to Hezbollah as an 'Islamist group'.

Right-Leaning outlets, however, frame Israel as 'Under Fire', portraying Hezbollah as a 'Militia' explicitly linked to 'Iran's Side in the War' and an 'Iranian-backed terror organization', highlighting Israel's 'offensive battle' and 'retaliatory strikes'.
The right also asserts Hezbollah 'joins war on Israel' and violated a 'fragile ceasefire', reporting 149 wounded in addition to 31 dead.
Center-Leaning perspectives focus on mutual 'exchange fire' and the 'conflict widens after attack on Iran' involving 'Iranian-backed militias'.

While all agree on evacuations , rocket fire, and 31 fatalities, the stark divergence in attributing motives, framing Israeli actions, and emphasizing geopolitical links reveals deep ideological divides regarding regional conflict and national security.
summary: On Monday, Israel and U.S. forces struck Iranian missile sites and navy targets, with officials claiming they destroyed leadership headquarters and warships as Iran and Iranian-backed militias fired missiles in response.
The joint America-Israeli attack on Sunday that killed Ali Khamenei, Supreme Leader, prompted Iran to fire missiles in response.

Health and military reports detail deaths, injuries and strike methods: the Health Ministry reported 149 wounded, and U.S. B-2 stealth bombers struck ballistic missile facilities with 2,000-pound bombs.
Gulf Arab states warned they could retaliate, and the WHO urged protection of civilians and healthcare facilities, as regional conflict escalates on Monday.

Combat leaders say operations will continue until objectives are met, and top Iranian security officials vowed they will not negotiate with the U.S., while Trump urged Iranians to 'take over' their government.
title: Israeli Attacks Kill at Least 31 in Lebanon as Iranian Attacks Continue Around Middle East

summary: On Sunday, US President Donald Trump urged Hamas to pursue disarmament and said the Board of Peace pledged $5 billion and personnel for stabilization.

The second phase of the US-brokered ceasefire plan stipulates Hamas must disarm, with Israeli forces withdrawing and an International Stabilization Force deployed, though Hamas calls disarmament a red line.
UN records show the plan was endorsed in November, and Trump said the Board of Peace will work with the United Nations; its charter does not seem limited to Gaza rebuilding oversight.

Requests for membership fees and invitations have prompted criticism as countries were asked to pay $1 billion for permanent membership, with France, Britain, and others expressing doubts amid ceasefire violations.
After meeting on the sidelines of the World Economic Forum in Davos last month, the Board of Peace is due to meet on Feb 19 in the US capital.
title: Trump tells Hamas to proceed with ‘Full and Immediate’ disarmament

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the planned prosecution of an Israeli settler for killing a Palestinian activist by emphasizing systemic injustice, detailing a "settler incursion" and the settler "wildly firing his gun after antagonising locals.
They highlight the family's view that the "manslaughter" charge "doesn’t rise to the level of the crime" and contextualize it with statistics on nationalist violence.
Conversely, right-leaning coverage presents the event with a more neutral tone, focusing on the legal process and individual accountability, de-emphasizing broader context.
Center-Leaning reports note the "Rare Prosecution Sparks Debate.
All perspectives acknowledge the rarity of such prosecutions and a spike in settler attacks, yet their divergent portrayals reveal underlying ideological priorities regarding the conflict.
summary: On Monday, Israel 's State Attorney General's office said it had initiated proceedings to indict Yinon Levi, an Israeli settler, over the killing of a Palestinian activist, opening a rare prosecution of settler violence.

Amid a sharp rise in settler attacks over two years, Hathaleen's death last July drew attention due to his role in the 2025 Oscar-winning film No Other Land and his funeral in Umm al-Khair on Aug. 7, 2025.

Multiple camera angles captured the confrontation in Umm al-Khair, including footage Alaa Hathaleen filmed showing Yinon Levi waving a pistol, firing two shots, shoving people, and later being detained, placed under house arrest and released.

Eitan Peleg, an attorney for Hathaleen's family, said prosecutors planned to indict Levi for reckless homicide, which the family called insufficient and described as an intentional, premeditated killing, a rare legal step in West Bank cases.
On Sunday, Israel's Foreign Ministry announced a land-registration process, with rights groups warning it could strip Palestinians of land and drawing condemnation from Saudi Arabia and Egypt.
title: Prosecutors plan to charge an Israeli settler with killing a Palestinian activist in the West Bank

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the exam postponement around "escalating tensions" or "conflict," while center outlets adopt a broader "geopolitical tensions" perspective, highlighting "student and staff safety.
Right-Leaning narratives, however, employ alarmist language like "chaos," " war ," and "Alert," creating a "Shadow of War" atmosphere.

This perspective uniquely emphasizes the profound human impact, detailing how "tens of thousands of Indian expat students" "grapple with chaos" and "hunker down" amidst disruptions, portraying exams as "pivotal" gateways—a focus de-emphasized by others.
While all agree on the core postponement, the stark divergence in emotional tone and emphasis on personal hardship reveals distinct ideological priorities in reporting geopolitical instability.
summary: CBSE postponed exams in Bahrain, Iran, Kuwait, Oman, Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the UAE, affecting Classes 10 and 12 board examinations, with new dates to be announced.

Reports said a US‑Israel offensive on Iran caused extensive damage and leadership casualties, leading to Iranian ballistic missile barrages that triggered civil‑defense alerts and regional airspace closures.

After a viral fake circular, CBSE clarified that its directory shows a Kendriya Vidyalaya in Tehran and 22 in Kuwait, 20 in Qatar, 41 in Saudi Arabia, eight in Bahrain, and 110 in the UAE, while informing Indian ambassadors in the affected countries.
title: India’s CBSE postpones class 10 and 12 board exams in Middle East; New dates to be announced

bias: While all perspectives acknowledge an "extraordinary meeting" on Iran , left-leaning coverage emphasizes Russia's "key ally" role in requesting it.
Center outlets attribute the meeting solely to "attacks by Israel and the US on Iran," de-emphasizing Russia's involvement.

Right-Leaning narratives employ alarmist terms like "Emergency Alert" and "Urgent Meeting," highlighting "Iran attacks" and "escalating conflict," uniquely detailing US-Israeli strikes' alleged aim at Tehran's atomic program and the IAEA's lack of radiological impact.

This divergence, from diplomatic context to immediate military threat and specific technical findings, underscores ideological splits on geopolitical blame and perceived security risks, despite agreement on the meeting's occurrence.
summary: On Monday , the Vienna-based IAEA will hold an extraordinary session on Iran, as requested by Russia and Iran itself, following recent strikes.
Russia requested the session, and Iran also wrote to IAEA chief Rafael Grossi seeking the meeting after the strikes, according to the IAEA's Saturday statement.
That extraordinary meeting will precede an already scheduled session of the board, which represents 35 countries, and the IAEA said it is closely monitoring developments in West Asia .
The IAEA stressed the `utmost urgency` to verify Iran's nuclear material, while the United States and Israel accuse Iran of seeking nuclear weapons.
Tehran denies military ambitions and insists on civilian nuclear rights, and the session follows U.S.-Israeli strikes Reuters said targeted Tehran's atomic programme.
title: U.N. nuclear agency to hold extraordinary meeting on Iran on March 2

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the U.S.-Israel action against Iran with terms like "fallout" and "attack," emphasizing "protection of civilians" and Canada 's de-emphasized military role.
Conversely, right-leaning outlets highlight a "coordinated operation" and "decisive support" from allies, portraying Canada's backing as "pragmatic diplomacy" and a significant relationship shift.
Center-Leaning reports neutrally convey official statements, like Carney "backs" Trump, and even include unverified claims regarding the Supreme Leader.

While all agree on the U.S.-Israel action and Canada's support to prevent Iran's nuclear weapon acquisition, these portrayals reveal underlying ideological divides between humanitarian caution and assertive foreign policy.
summary: On Saturday, Prime Minister Mark Carney said Canada supports the United States acting to prevent Iran obtaining a nuclear weapon but does not envision joining military operations.
Targeting Iranian leadership, the strikes on Saturday followed, with the U.S. and Israel launching coordinated attacks after months of planning, officials said.

Global Affairs Canada updated travel advisories, warning of ongoing military activity, and advised Canadians in Iran to shelter or consider leaving by commercial carriers while options remain, noting limited consular support.

Iran retaliated with missile and drone strikes targeting Israel and countries hosting U.S. troops, while explosions and smoke were reported across Tehran and state media said a strike on a girls' school in southern Iran killed at least 51 students.
Amid international alarm, Prime Minister Mark Carney urged all sides to protect civilians in Mumbai and Canada said it is closely following Iran-related hostilities.
title: Carney says Canada supports U.S. action in Iran but won’t get involved militarily

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the IRGC claim that four ballistic missiles "struck" the USS Abraham Lincoln as part of a tit‑for‑tat escalation, stressing uncertainty with phrases like "unverified" and "impact not immediately confirmed," while right-leaning pieces foreground retaliation rhetoric—"revenge," "retaliation," "WW3 fears"—and often amplify the attack narrative even as they repeat CENTCOM's dismissal that the missiles "didn't even come close.
A key dividing line is portrayal of motive versus verification ; center coverage privileges official denials and casualty counts .
Both sides agree on the claims, the denial, and the regional escalation, reflecting broader ideological and security-oriented rifts.
summary: The IRGC claimed on Sunday that it struck the USS Abraham Lincoln, U.S. Navy aircraft carrier, with four ballistic missiles, but U.S. Central Command called the claim a 'lie' and said the missiles didn't come close.
Iran says its recent strikes followed Saturday and is retaliation for U.S.-Israeli attacks, targeting Gulf states and U.S. bases, including the USS Abraham Lincoln.
Operating off Oman, the USS Abraham Lincoln is a nuclear-powered, fifth Nimitz-class aircraft carrier homeported in San Diego, California, launching strikes under Operation Epic Fury.
Reported casualties include three U.S. service members killed and five seriously wounded, with Gulf cities experiencing civilian casualties and airport closures.

The United Nations held an emergency meeting and called for de-escalation as Israel warned of more strikes, describing the episode as the first direct attack on a U.S. aircraft carrier in decades, raising regional escalation concerns.
title: Iran’s Guards say they ‘struck’ US aircraft carrier USS Abraham Lincoln
WSJ: Data Centers Unplugging at Once Pose New Threat to US Power Grids

bias: Left and right-leaning outlets converge on the indictment of two men for firearm charges following a Utah church parking lot shooting, yet diverge significantly in framing.

The left and right emphasize the individuals as "alleged gang members," with the right further highlighting the "church" context and "funeral shooting" to evoke societal concerns, even including an alias like "aka 'Lost One'.
In contrast, center-leaning coverage de-emphasizes gang affiliation, strictly presenting specific names and precise legal charges.

This reveals a partisan divide where the left and right lean into characterizations and emotional framing, while the center prioritizes unadorned legal details, reflecting broader ideological differences in crime reporting.
summary: A federal grand jury in Salt Lake City indicted Ryan Daniel Toutai and Fineeva Pomona Maka on firearms charges tied to a Jan. 7, 2026 shooting that left two dead and six injured.
At the meetinghouse, authorities found the shooting erupted during a funeral involving Tongan congregants, following a dispute among attendees who knew each other.

Investigators recovered a Ruger 9mm pistol,.40 caliber and 9mm casings, and seized Toutai's phone showing photos of the firearm's serial number and GPS data linking Maka to the weapon on January 7, 2026.
They are scheduled to appear in court on March 3, 2026, and Toutai was previously arrested last month for obstruction of justice , the U.S. Attorney's Office said.

No one has yet been charged in the uncharged homicides, a third arrested individual faces related charges, and the shooting at a church serving Tongan congregants heightens local community impact where more than 25% of the U.S. Tongan population resides in Utah.
title: 2 indicted on firearm charges in connection with shooting outside Utah church

Oil jumps 10% on Iran conflict and could spike to $100 a barrel, analysts say
